entry.title: Enhancing SAP IBP Integration: Evolution and Innovation
entry.content:
At SAP, we're committed to providing our customers with cutting-edge solutions that streamline operations and reduce costs. We're excited to share some important updates about our SAP IBP integration offerings.  

Continuity for CI-DS Users 

First and foremost, we want to reassure IBP customers using CI-DS: there are no plans to sunset or discontinue the CI-DS integration. With all SAP IBP customers relying on SAP CI-DS, we remain committed to supporting and maintaining this integration method with Bug fixes and security related enhancements. 

Behind-the-Scenes Improvements 

While maintaining continuity for our users, we're also working on enhancing the underlying infrastructure. The CI-DS infrastructure is transitioning from the SAP NEO cloud infrastructure to a SAP BTP Kyma-based infrastructure. This change happens behind the scenes, ensuring that end-users won't experience any changes in system look, feel, or behavior. 

Exciting New Integration Option Coming in H2-2025 

We're thrilled to announce that in the second half of 2025, we'll be introducing a new integration option that promises faster and tighter integration with SAP ERP and S/4HANA On Premise and Private Cloud systems. This new feature will utilize SAP real-time integration and the Cloud Integration capability of SAP Integration Suite.  
SAP real-time integration will seamlessly integrate master, order, and stock data, while the Cloud Integration capability of SAP Integration Suite will allow for the transfer of Key Figure data and offer flexibility to connect to other SAP systems, third-party, legacy systems, databases, and files. 

Key Benefits of the New Integration: 

  1. Seamless and Flexible Integration: Customers will enjoy a more streamlined integration process with SAP ERP systems. 
  2. Out-of-the-Box Integration: Predefined content will make setup and maintenance easier than ever. 
  3. The Integration Suite's broad capabilities and use cases across SAP S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Concur, Ariba, and more enable consolidation into a single middleware, reducing the time, cost, and effort required to integrate diverse business systems 

What This Means for You 

Existing CI-DS users can confidently continue with their current integration methods. For those considering SAP IBP or seeking to optimize their integration processes, the forthcoming SAP real-time integration and SAP Integration Suite Cloud Integration option will offer an efficient and cost-effective solution. Currently, customers can access selected integration packages for SAP IBP with S/4 Public Cloud, Ariba, Everstream, and SAP SAC through the Cloud Integration capability of SAP Integration Suite. 

Looking Ahead 

These developments reflect our ongoing commitment to innovation and customer satisfaction. By offering multiple integration options, we're ensuring that businesses can choose the solution that best fits their unique requirements and infrastructure. 

We're excited about these advancements and look forward to seeing how they will benefit our customers' operations. As we approach the launch date for the new SAP Integration Suite Cloud Integration integration option, we'll provide more detailed information and guidance. 

Additional information:

  • https://www.sap.com/canada/products/technology-platform/integration-suite.html 
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/what-is-sap-cloud-integration
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite 
  • https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/serviceCatalog/integration-suite?region=all

entry.title: Integrating Netweaver ≤7.4 Systems with SAP Forms Service by Adobe on Cloud Foundry
entry.content:
Problem Statement

If you are planning to operate your Netweaver ≤7.4 systems with SAP Forms Service by Adobe until the end of the Netweaver's Extended Maintenance in 2030, you might have encountered the problem that these Netweaver releases are only compatible with the SAP Forms Service by Adobe on BTP Neo, which will be sunset end of 2028. To make things worse, SAP Forms Service by Adobe on BTP Cloud Foundry is only compatible with Netweaver 7.5 and S/4HANA Foundation systems.

Does that mean that you have to upgrade to Netweaver 7.5 to use SAP Forms Service by Adobe after 2028?

Before 2028, does that mean that you have to operate two instances of SAP Forms Service by Adobe in parallel (one on BTP Neo, one on BTP Cloud Foundry), if you have both Netweaver ≤7.4 systems and Netweaver 7.5 or S/4HANA Foundation systems in your landscape?

Fortunately, the incompatibility is only caused by the authentication mechanism. In this blog, we will cover the steps to integrate Netweaver ≤7.4 system with SAP Forms Service by Adobe on BTP Cloud Foundry by means of a middleware (e.g. SAP Integration Suite, SAP PI, or any other) that supports OAuth 2.0 authentication. This way, you will only need to operate SAP Forms Service by Adobe on BTP Cloud Foundry and you avoid an upgrade to Netweaver 7.5, if all you want is to run out the clock until your S/4 Transformation is finished.

 

 

Solution Scenarios & Benefit

This guide is hence particularly useful for customers in these scenarios:

  • No plans to upgrade to ECC 6.0 EhP 8 (i.e. NW 7.5)
  • Plans to operate on ECC 6.0 EhP 7 (i.e. NW 7.4) past 2028 towards 2030
  • Hybrid landscape with legacy ECC and S/4 workloads requiring PDF generation

The benefits of this custom approach include:

  • A unified setup that supports both legacy ECC EhP≤7/NW≤7.4 and new S/4 workloads
  • Reduced infrastructure and support costs
  • Elimination of the need to manually apply security patches

 

 

Feature Comparison of SAP Forms Service by Adobe:
BTP Neo vs. BTP Cloud Foundry

Before we commence with the configuration steps, let's compare the features of Forms Service on Neo and CloudFoundry environments.


 SAP Forms Service by Adobe
on BTP Neo		SAP Forms Service by Adobe
on BTP Cloud Foundry
CompatibilityABAP Netweaver 7.0 SP13 up to Netweaver 7.4

S/4 HANA version 1809 (SP8+), 1909 (SP6+), 2020 (SP4+), 2021, or higher.

Netweaver version 7.50 SP24 or higher

AuthenticationBasic AuthenticationOAuth 2.0
Cloud InfrastructureEnd of life by 2028Future-proof

 

 

Architecture & Solution Concept

Since the incompatibility between Netweaver ≤7.4 and SAP Forms Service by Adobe on BTP Cloud Foundry is caused by the authentication method (at an identical payload), compatibility can be reestablished by introduction of a middleware that employs BasicAuth towards the Netweaver ≤7.4 system and OAuth 2.0 towards SAP Forms Service by Adobe on BTP Cloud Foundry while forwarding the payload in both directions.

 

BTP_Forms_Service

 

 

Configuration

1. Middleware
(Integration Suite)

  • Configure the "HTTPS" sender adapter which acts as a proxy to Forms service on Cloud Foundry.
  • Value configured in "Address" field should be used as "Path Prefix" in ADS RFC destination which will be configured later in ABAP system.

Sender_Adapter

  • Configure the "HTTP" receiver adapter to forward the request to Forms service on Cloud Foundry. This adapter will use OAuth 2.0 authentication as required for Forms Service.
    • Generate the service key for Forms service instance before configuring this step.
  • Fill in the following information:
    • Address: uri from service key/AdobeDocumentServicesSec/Config
    • Query: style=rpc
    • Proxy Type: Internet
    • Method: POST
    • Authentication: OAuth2 Client Credentials
    • Credential Name: <Name of secret configured in Integration Suite>

Receiver_Adapter

  • Deploy the artifact.

Full_Integration

 

 

2. Netweaver

  • Update "ADS" RFC in SM59.
    • Host: <Hostname of the middleware>
    • Port: 443
    • Path Prefix: <address configured in Sender adapter>

SM59_1

  • In "Logon & Security" tab update the authentication details.
    • Select "Basic Authentication"
    • User: <provide the Client ID from Service Key generated in Integration suite>
    • Password: <provide the Client secret from Service Key generated in Integration suite>
    • SSL: set to "Active"
    • SSL certificate: select the "SSL Client Anonymous" PSE
      • Before using the PSE, Open https://www.digicert.com/digicert-root-certificates.htm in your web browser and download the following certificates:
        • DigiCert Global Root CA
        • DigiCert Global Root G2
        • ISRG Root X1 and ISRG Root X2 (available for download from Chains of Trust - Let's Encrypt).
      • Use transaction STRUSTSSO2 to import the downloaded certificates to SSL Client (Anonymous) PSE.

SM59_2

  • Save the RFC connection changes
  • Connection test will always end in "500 - Internal Server error" which is expected.

SM59_3

 

 

3. Cloud Connector

  • Open Cloud Connector Administration UI and configure new system mapping (Cloud to On-premise). For details, refer to Configure the Destination in the Cloud Connector | SAP Help Portal.
  • Connect the Cloud Connector with the "Forms Service" sub account provisioned on BTP.
  • After the system mapping is configured, select the row and Choose Add (+) in the Resources Accessible On... section.
    • Add /sap/bc/fp/ (subpaths included) and
    • add /sap/bc/fpads/ (subpaths included).

Connector_1

 

 

4. SAP Forms Service by Adobe

  • Login to BTP Forms service subaccount and configure the destination to ABAP system, destination name should follow this naming convention FP_ICF_DATA_<ABAP SID>.
    • Type: HTTP
    • URL: http://<virtual host name configured in cloud connector>:<virtual port configured in cloud connector>
    • Proxy Type: OnPremise
    • Authentication: BasicAuthentication
    • Location ID(Optional): Enter location ID if configured in Cloud Connector
    • User: System user in ABAP system (Ensure the user has proper authorization. Refer note 3406470)
    • Password: Password of System user
  • In Additional properties section, add New Property
    • sap-client: Client number of ABAP system

BTP_Forms

  • Save and test the connection.

BTP_ConnectionTest

 

 

5. Test PDF generation

You can generate test PDFs using standard report FP_TEST_00 available in ABAP system.

FP_TEST_00 report SE38

This should successfully conclude the configuration enabling your Netweaver ≤7.4-based system to utilize SAP Forms Service by Adobe on BTP Cloud Foundry.

entry.title: Generation of Message Processing logs report for EDI transactions using SAP CI.
entry.content:
Here the requirement to generate the report on ad-hoc basis to check the Orders, Order Acknowledgements and Invoices status based upon the trading partner number in EDI integration. In day-to-day basis customers use to send 1000's of orders there is possibility of missing track of them. in spite of checking using message monitoring page by providing order numbers or trading partner numbers etc., I came across with a solution of using standard API provided by SAP by making some custom changes in the I-Flows of EDI and creating a separate flow for generating a report. Here are the below steps to follow to achieve this.

Firstly, add the custom header to I-Flow’s with your wish and add the Exchange Property as a X-Path and provide the x-path source value in the tab and provide the data type accordingly., here I am using trading partner number as the custom header for all EDI flow's.

Please check the below script for storing custom header

//

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;

//Add file name to custom header

def Message addFileName(Message message) {

    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message);

    if(messageLog != null){

        //Read FileName

                             def fileName = message.getProperty("EDI_TradingPartner");

                    messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("EDI_TradingPartner", fileName);

    }

    return message;

}

//

After adding these script and exchange property check that is getting reflected in message monitoring page as below.

We are good w.r.t integration flow design. Now we are good to start with creating an I-Flow for generating report for making our life easier.

Prior to creating the I-flow check the prerequisites.  To consume the standard API to get the message processing logs in SAP CPI, Create an Instance of Process Integration Runtime with Plan API in BTP cockpit of XXX subaccount and store the service key details for future reference. This will be used to extract the .edmx file of ODATA API.

Once the instance is created, please feel to store those credentials in security material and can be used in communication channel.

Create an Integration flow with the name as you wish, once I-flow is created is Please follow below steps.

  1. Create sender channel with https adapter and expose the endpoint by deploying the flow, this URL we will use for sending the request.

     

     Take a content modifier and create an Exchange Property with I-Flow Name Start date, configure source values and externalize them as we are going to change frequently in future.

  2. Now we have standard API in SAP Business Accelerator Hub with below details to extract all the values which is present in the message processing logs like MessageGuid, Correlation, ApplicationMessageId, etc., with below to get these details kindly download OData EDMX from API specification tab and add it to references in your flow as schema.

    Adding address for your reference: https://api.sap.com/api/MessageProcessingLogs/overview
  3.  Take the Request Reply pallet and connect to receiver with OData adapter and maintain below details in the connection parameters and processing steps.Address: https://{your CI tenanturldetails} /api/v1

    Proxy Type as internet and maintain the authentication and provide the authentication details which you have stored in security material.

  4. Now maintain the processing steps as below
    Operation Details : Query(GET)
    Resource Path : MessageProcessingLogs
    Query options : based up on your requirement by using preview steps.

     

  5. Once done with all these, store the required details in exchange property like Status, ApplicationMessageId, IntegrationFlowName and MessageGuid in a content modifier. Here MessageGuid plays a key role in getting the details of custom headers, this API does not provide the details of custom headers in response, to achieve this take one more request reply and send it to receiver via HTTP adapter. Since we have one more standard API for pulling custom headers as listed below.

     

     

  6. Now provide the necessary details in HTTP adapter like address and credentials as below reference.

     

  7. Now, take a content modifier and maintain the Custom Header Name and value in the exchange property to store incoming values and store all the properties in the message body as expression.

     

  8. Now we are almost there, take a gather step to gather all the information with below aggregation strategy.

     

  9. Now, we are in final step take an XML to CSV converter to see the report in CSV format with below configuration.

     

  10. We have completed all the steps in creating a flow and we are good to test and check for the outcome from Postman.

     

    Thank you everyone, I think this will be quite useful for all integration folks who are working with EDI transactions can make their job easy.

    Cheers and happy learning !!

entry.title: Integrating SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys via SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog post provides a step-by-step guide on integrating SAP Sales Cloud V2 with SAP Emarsys using the SAP Integration Suite. The focus is on setting up a straightforward integration flow (iFlow) to extract contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2 and create corresponding contacts in SAP Emarsys.

The goal is to demonstrate how to connect both systems, using APIs and middleware to automate data transfer. This serves as a foundation for more complex integrations in the future, making it easier to extend the process with additional functionality. A key aspect of this integration is OpenID Connect (OIDC, OAuth 2.0, JWT) based API authentication, which is required to securely access SAP Emarsys. We will cover how authentication works in this context and how to configure it properly within the iFlow.

 

Prerequisites

Before implementing this iFlow ensure that you have the following:

  • An active SAP Integration Suite tenant with Cloud Integration enabled
  • Access to a SAP Sales Cloud V2 account with API permissions
  • Access to an SAP Emarsys account with API permissions

 

Implementation

Overview of the Integration Flow

Overview of the iFlow between SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys using SAP Integration SuiteThe diagram above provides an overview of the iFlow, which connects SAP Sales Cloud V2 with SAP Emarsys using the Cloud Integration component within the SAP Integration Suite. This process automates retrieving contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2, processes the information, and transfers it to SAP Emarsys to create corresponding contacts.

  1. Start Timer – Initiates the iFlow execution at predefined intervals.
  2. Set Filter – Applies conditions to select only relevant contacts from SAP Sales Cloud V2.
  3. Get Sales Cloud Contacts – Fetches contact data via the SAP Sales Cloud API.
  4. Extract Sales Cloud Contacts – Processes and structures the extracted data.
  5. Configure SAP Emarsys Request –Prepares the Request and Setting Up OAuth Authentication
  6. Create Contacts in SAP Emarsys – Sends the contact data via an API request to SAP Emarsys.

In the following sections, each step of the iFlow will be explained in detail.

Step 1: Start Timer - Triggering the Integration Flow

Timer Configuration – Scheduling the iFlow to run at regular intervals

The iFlow is initiated by a timer-based trigger, which ensures that contact data is regularly synchronized between SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys.

As shown in the screenshot, the timer is configured using a Simple Schedule, where the iFlow runs every hour between February 27, 2025, at 09:00 UTC and March 6, 2025, at 18:00 UTC.

Step 2: Set Filter - Filtering Relevant Contacts

Configure SAP Sales Cloud V2 Request

In this step, we apply a filter to ensure that only the necessary contacts are fetched from SAP Sales Cloud V2. First, we limit the selection to contacts associated with the United States. Second, we include only those that have been newly created or updated within the last hour.

After adding the timer to trigger the iFlow, the next step is to insert a Content Modifier to define the query parameters for retrieving the relevant contact data.

  1. Add a Content Modifier
  2. Navigate to the Message Header tab.
  3. Create a new header entry:
    • Action: Create
    • Name: CamelHttpQuery (you can choose your own name)
    • Source Type: Expression
    • Source Value: $filter=isContactPersonFor/country eq 'US' and (adminData/createdOn gt '${date:now:yyyy-MM-dd}T${date:now:HH}:00:00Z' or adminData/updatedOn gt '${date:now:yyyy-MM-dd}T${date:now:HH}:00:00Z'})

The filter dynamically adjusts the timestamp to the beginning of the current hour, ensuring that each execution of the integration flow retrieves only the latest contact records.

Step 3: Get Sales Cloud Contacts - Fetching Data from SAP Sales Cloud V2

To retrieve contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2, we need to set up a Request Reply and connect it to an HTTP Receiver Adapter. This ensures that the iFlow sends a request to SAP Sales Cloud V2 and processes the response dynamically.

1. Setting Up the Request Reply Step

The Request Reply step is required to handle synchronous communication with SAP Sales Cloud. It ensures that the request is sent, and the response is processed before moving to the next step in the iFlow.

  1. Add a Request Reply step after the Content Modifier.
  2. Connect the Request Reply step to an HTTP Receiver Adapter.

2. Configuring the HTTP Receiver Adapter

The HTTP Receiver Adapter is responsible for sending the request to SAP Sales Cloud V2. The configuration is as follows:

Configure the connection to SAP Sales Cloud V2

  • Address: The endpoint for retrieving contact persons: 
    https://mytenant.crm.cloud.sap/sap/c4c/api/v1/contact-person-service/contactPersons
  • Method: GET is selected to fetch data.
  • Proxy Type: Set to Internet, ensuring external API communication.
  • Authentication: Basic
  • Credentials: Since SAP Sales Cloud V2 requires authentication, we must configure Basic Authentication in the Security Material section of the SAP Integration Suite.
    • Set up Basic Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite: Go to "Monitor" → "Security Material" → Create a new "User Credentials" → enter a Credential Name, Username and Password → add the Credential Name in HTTP Receiver Adapter

The dynamic filter query that was set up in the Content Modifier is passed automatically in the request to ensure only relevant contact data is retrieved.

Step 4: Extract Sales Cloud Contacts - Extracting Contact Information

In this step, we process the contact data retrieved from SAP Sales Cloud V2, extract the relevant fields, and format them for further use in the integration. To achieve this, we use a Groovy script that structures the data according to SAP Emarsys' requirements. For simplicity, this example extracts only the first name, last name, and email address, but additional fields can be included as needed.

 

 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper;
import groovy.json.JsonOutput;

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String)  
    def jsonSlurper = new JsonSlurper()
    def jsonResponse = jsonSlurper.parseText(body)  

    if (!jsonResponse || !jsonResponse.value || jsonResponse.value.isEmpty()) {
        throw new RuntimeException("No contact data found. API Response: " + body)
    }

    def contactsList = jsonResponse.value.collect { contact ->
        return [
            "3": contact.eMail ?: "",  
            "1": contact.givenName ?: "",  
            "2": contact.familyName ?: ""  
        ]
    }

    def emarsysPayload = [
        key_id: "3",
        contacts: contactsList
    ]

    message.setProperty("EmarsysBody", JsonOutput.toJson(emarsysPayload))  // Store entire JSON in a property

    return message;
}

 

We start by retrieving the API response as a string and parsing it into a JSON object. If no contacts are found, we throw an exception to prevent unnecessary API calls.

Next, we extract the key attributes needed for SAP Emarsys. In this example, we focus on:

  • Email Address (3)
  • First Name (1)
  • Last Name (2)

We iterate over the contact list and map each contact’s attributes into a structured format. If any of these fields are missing, we assign an empty string to ensure consistency in the data structure.

Once we have extracted the required fields, we structure the data into a JSON payload that SAP Emarsys can process. Since SAP Emarsys uses the email address as the unique identifier, we set the key_id to 3

Finally, we store the structured JSON in a message property (EmarsysBody), making it available for the next step in the iFlow.

Step 5: Configure SAP Emarsys Request

In this step, we prepare the data extracted from SAP Sales Cloud V2 for submission to the SAP Emarsys API. This involves setting up a Content Modifier and configuring the necessary OpenID Connect (OIDC, OAuth 2.0, JWT) based API authentication using the Integration Suite.

1. Add a Content Modifier

First, add a Content Modifier step in the iFlow after the Groovy script. The purpose of this step is to store the payload that will be sent to the SAP Emarsys API.

Before creating the new headers, delete the existing CamelHttpQuery header to avoid conflicts with the new request configuration. To do this, add a new row in the Message Header section and select Delete as the action. Enter CamelHttpQuery as the header name and leave the other fields empty.

Message Header

HTTP headers for Emarsys request and OAuth authentication

Next, we need to add the required headers for the OAuth 2.0 request and data exchange with the SAP Emarsys API:

  • Go to the Message Header tab.
  • Add first header:
    • Name: Content-Type
    • Source Type: Constant
    • Source Value: application/json
  • Add second header:
    • Name: Accept
    • Source Type: Constant
    • Source Value: application/json
Message Body

Next, we need to define the message body that will be sent to the SAP Emarsys API. This body contains the structured contact data extracted and formatted in the Groovy script:

  • Navigate to the Message Body tab.
  • Type: Expression 
  • Body: ${property.EmarsysBody} - we set this property in the groovy script.

2. Configure OAuth 2.0 Authentication

To connect to the SAP Emarsys API, you need to configure OAuth 2.0 authentication in the Integration Suite. This is done by setting up OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials in the Security Material.

Configuring Security Material for Emarsys Authentication

You can obtain the OAuth 2.0 credentials directly from SAP Emarsys or configure them using OpenID Connect with SAP Cloud Identity. For detailed steps on setting up credentials via OpenID Connect, refer to the official  SAP Emarsys documentation here.

Alternatively, you can create API credentials directly in SAP Emarsys. You can find the detailed steps here. Ensure that the Token Service URL is correctly set to the SAP Emarsys token endpoint.

When configuring OAuth 2.0 credentials in the Security Material of the Integration Suite, make sure to populate the SCOPE field (e.g.'api'). Missing scope values can lead to authentication errors. For more details on setting up OAuth 2.0 authentication, refer to this SAP Community blog.

Step 6: Create Contacts in SAP Emarsys

In this step, we create new contacts in SAP Emarsys using the extracted and formatted data from SAP Sales Cloud V2. To achieve this, we need to configure a Request Reply Step and an HTTP Receiver Adapter to send the contact data to SAP Emarsys.

1. Setting Up the Request Reply Step

  1. Add a Request Reply after the Content Modifier.
  2. Connect the Request Reply to an HTTP Receiver Adapter.

2. Configuring the HTTP Receiver Adapter

  • Address: The endpoint for creating contacts in SAP Emarsys: https://api.emarsys.net/api/v3/contact
  • Proxy Type: Internet
  • Method: POST
  • Authentication: OAuth2 Client Credentials
  • Credential Name: Your credential name from the previous step

At this point, the contact data extracted from SAP Sales Cloud V2 is ready to be created in SAP Emarsys. The HTTP Receiver Adapter sends the formatted data as a POST request to the SAP Emarsys API.

 

Validating the Integration

After setting up the iFlow and configuring all necessary steps, we can now test the integration to confirm that contact data is successfully transferred from SAP Sales Cloud V2 to SAP Emarsys.

Step 1: Create a Contact in SAP Sales Cloud V2

To validate the integration, we start by creating a new contact in SAP Sales Cloud V2. In this example, a contact named Emma Evans with the email address emma.evans@alpha.cx.demo.sap was created in SAP Sales Cloud V2.

Step 2: Verify That the Contact is Not Yet Available in SAP Emarsys

Next, we search for the newly created contact in SAP Emarsys using the email address. As shown in the screenshot, the search returns no results, confirming that the contact is not yet present in SAP Emarsys.

Step 3: Execute the Integration Flow

We then manually trigger the iFlow to push the newly created contact from SAP Sales Cloud V2 to SAP Emarsys. The iFlow sends a POST request to the SAP Emarsys API with the contact details extracted and formatted in the previous steps.

Step 4: Confirm That the Contact is Now Available in SAP Emarsys

Finally, we search for the contact in SAP Emarsys again using the email address. This time, the contact is successfully found, confirming that the data transfer was successful. The contact's first name, last name, and email address are correctly displayed in SAP Emarsys, indicating that the iFlow is working as expected.

 

Conclusion

In this post, we walked through the process of setting up an end-to-end integration between SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys using the Cloud Integration component within the SAP Integration Suite. We started by configuring the connection to SAP Sales Cloud V2, filtering the data to retrieve only relevant contacts, and extracting the necessary fields using a Groovy script. We then prepared the data for SAP Emarsys, set up OpenID Connect (OIDC, OAuth 2.0, JWT) based API authentication, and sent the data to SAP Emarsys using an HTTP Receiver Adapter.

The successful execution of the integration was confirmed when the contact created in SAP Sales Cloud V2 appeared in SAP Emarsys after the process ran. This demonstrates how easily the SAP Integration Suite can be used to connect SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys - laying the foundation for more complex processes in the future. 

By following these steps, you can build a similar integration in your environment, adapting the filter conditions and field mapping to meet your specific business needs.

entry.title: Webinar: Beyond Integration - How PepsiCo Builds Custom HR Applications with SAP BTP
entry.content:
Many organizations have a need to build custom HR applications and extensions to complement their SAP SuccessFactors solutions. As part of Comentec’s “SAP BTP for HR” webinar series, this episode moves beyond integration to explore the SAP Business Technology Platform's full potential for HR solutions.

Please join SAP BTP and SAP SuccessFactors partner, Comentec and PepsiCo's HR Technology team to demonstrate how SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) enables HR and HRIS to efficiently create high-quality custom HR extensions with limited development effort.

Using a Talent Card application as an example, Comentec will showcase how BTP's built-in templates, standards, and security features are combined to create robust solutions that HR technology teams can easily maintain while PepsiCo will share their firsthand experience of using BTP to develop a secure, sustainable HR application that utilizes data from SAP SuccessFactors.

When:  Date: April 2, 2025. |  Time: 11:30AM US EST, 17:30 CET
Registration Link

Join us to discover how SAP BTP's standardized components and best practices can help you:

  • Develop custom applications for SAP SuccessFactors with reduced effort and complexity
  • Create solutions that are easier to maintain and support internally
  • Leverage SAP's standard technology stack for improved sustainability
  • Build a stronger business case for custom application development

This session will help you reimagine the possibilities of BTP and provide you with the confidence to evaluate how it can be leveraged across your project roadmap.

This webinar is designed for:

  • Strategic decision-makers evaluating Employee Central and extensibility options
  • Technical leaders managing existing HR environment seeking modernization strategies
  • Business stakeholders wanting to improve data access, user experience, and HR processes.

Event info:
Date: April 2, 2025.
Time: 11:30AM US EST, 17:30 CET
Registration Link- this will take you to the registration page hosted by Comentec.

SAP Build Apps SAP Build Code SAP Business Technology Platform

entry.title: SAP Code Jam Sessions in Risch-Rotkreuz
entry.content:
Lust auf spannende Hands-on-Erfahrungen mit den neuesten SAP-Technologien? Dann sei dabei, wenn im Mai zwei SAP Code Jam Events in Risch-Rotkreuz stattfinden!

Themen

  • SAP Build - Event-based Processes with SAP Build Process Automation
  • Event-driven integration - Using SAP Integration Suite, advanced event-mesh

Was erwartet dich?

  • Intensive, praxisnahe Sessions mit Experten der SAP
  •  Hands-on Coding an realen Szenarien
  • Austausch mit Gleichgesinnten
  • Aktuelle Technologien & Best Practices

Event Rahmenbedingungen

  • Datum: Mittwoch, 21. Mai & Donnerstag, 22. Mai 2025
  • Zeit: 09:00 - 17:00 Uhr
  • Ort: GAMBIT Consulting AG, Suurstoffi 22, 6343 Risch-Rotkreuz
  • Sprache: Englisch

Ob Einsteiger oder Developer mit grossem Erfahrungsschatz – diese Sessions bieten eine grossartige Gelegenheit, dein Wissen zu vertiefen und neue SAP Features auszuprobieren.

Melde dich jetzt an!

  • SAP Build - Event-based Processes with SAP Build Process Automation
  • Event-driven integration - Using SAP Integration Suite, advanced event-mesh

Wir freuen uns auf dich!

Deine SAP Community Luzern

entry.title: Enhancing Business Agility with Enterprise Automation: SAP Build and SAP Signavio
entry.content:
The ability to intelligently automate business processes is top of mind for many stakeholders involved in process management across organizations. According to a recent IDC study, annual spending on Intelligent Process Automation (IPA) technologies is projected to reach $102 billion by 2028. Of this, $19.9 billion will be allocated to AI-powered automation tools, with an impressive annual growth rate of 43.4%.

While there will be certainly no shortage in resources to spend for automation software, the real challenge remains in the details: How can organizations implement a holistic approach for process excellence, which allows them to manage processes, identify bottlenecks, deliver scalable automations and integrate their process extension within a holistic process landscape. In the previous blogpost, @Manuel_Namyslo has already outlined in detail how SAP’s framework on Enterprise Automation delivers an solution to the problem statement mentioned above.

And this blogpost series will offer you a more detailed guidance on the interconnectivity of each service included in Enterprise Automation which are:

  • SAP Build Process Automation
  • SAP Integration Suite
  • and, of course, SAP Signavio.

Manuel has already published a blog on the joint usage patterns for SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite in the first blogpost of the series.

Let’s explore the value you can unlock for your processes by combining SAP Build Process Automation with SAP Signavio in this one.

What is Enterprise Automation?

Enterprise automation with SAP software provides an integrated set of tools for process mining, automation, integration, monitoring, and optimization of your processes and workflows. It helps you quickly and intuitively gain insights into your business processes and identify the right automation tool for the right outcome. While each solution can be leveraged independently, Enterprise Automation offer native integration between SAP Build, SAP integration Suite and SAP Signavio – supplemented with best practices, guidance and pre-build content which can be implemented directly.

  • SAP Signavio provides visibility into business processes and potential bottlenecks, root-cause analysis, and suggestions for improvement based on 200,000 industry benchmarks and best practices. This detailed understanding of inefficiencies across your organization enables you to optimize and automate processes.
  • SAP Integration Suite is an integration platform as a service with thousands of prebuilt integrations, APIs, events and connectors to SAP and third-party software applications. The suite reduces complexity across on-premise and multi-cloud environments, helping you optimize comprehensive business processes.
  • SAP Build to create automations and applications using the low-code and no-code design tools. Including pre-built automations and connectors for SAP software and third-party systems, the solutions let your business experts rapidly address process inefficiencies.

Leverage the combined power of SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Signavio

1. Process Recommendations

Every organization runs complex business processes, from procurement to order management and HR workflows. However, inefficiencies, bottlenecks, and compliance gaps often remain hidden within these processes, leading to delays and unnecessary costs.

SAP Signavio Process Insights is a lightweight solution designed to help businesses analyze and optimize their operational processes. It provides data-driven insights by identifying inefficiencies, automation opportunities, as well as performance bottlenecks, and can be leveraged alongside your SAP S/4HANA data. The tool enables companies to benchmark their processes against industry best practices, prioritize improvement initiatives, and accelerate digital transformation. By offering predefined process performance indicators and process recommendations, SAP Signavio Process Insights helps organizations enhance efficiency, reduce costs, and drive continuous improvement in their business operations.

However, identifying issues is just the first step—organizations need a way to act on these insights and implement improvements. This is where SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and SAP Build come into play.

SAP Signavio Process Insights can recommend specific pre-built content, tailored to the process specifics. This content is available to be deployed with SAP Build Process Automation, so companies can directly implement these packages to make their scenarios executable.

2. From Insights to Action

Having the recommendations of what can be used is a great option to start. But there is more to the story. Organizations need a seamless way to make the outcomes of the process analysis actionable and continuously optimize their operations. This is where SAP Signavio Process Intelligence and SAP Build Process Automation come together to drive meaningful transformation.

SAP Signavio Process Intelligence enables organizations to perform a full-fledged process mining, including:

  • Analysis process data extracted from SAP S/4HANA, SAP ECC, and other connected systems.
  • Detection of inefficiencies, such as approval delays, reworks needed or missed SLAs.
  • Visualization of process flows with its deviations, conformance and variants.

This insight provides a clear roadmap for improvement—but how do we act on it efficiently?

SAP Build Process Automation bridges the gap by enabling organizations to implement improvements with minimal IT effort.

Using the combination of the tools, businesses can define conditions and triggers based on findings from SAP Signavio Process Intelligence and trigger an approval flow or a task automation scenario (e.g. an exception flow) directly using the native API connectivity.

For example, if Process Intelligence detects a high percentage of purchase orders stuck in approval, SAP Build Process Automation can automatically trigger a workflow that escalates delays, notifies the right stakeholders, or reroutes tasks to speed up processing.

Now, optimization is not a one-time effort—it requires continuous monitoring and refinement. Once automated workflows are in place, organizations must track their performance and fine-tune processes as needed.

With SAP Signavio Process Intelligence, businesses can:

  • Measure the impact of automation by tracking KPIs before and after implementation.
  • Identify new bottlenecks or areas for further automation in a cycle of continuous improvement, receiving the data from the executed workflow.
  • Refine processes dynamically, ensuring agility and efficiency in ever-changing business conditions.

3. Model-to-Execute: BPMN Import for Executable Processes

In today's dynamic business environment, organizations need to rapidly transform business processes from concept to execution. One of the key challenges has been the disconnect between process modeling and process automation—where business analysts model processes that then need to be separately implemented by technical teams, often leading to misalignment and delays.

SAP is looking to address this challenge through a BPMN Import capability, a roadmap item for SAP Build Process Automation, bridging the gap between process design and execution.

Traditionally, business processes follow this journey:

  1. Business analysts model best practice processes, what can be done using SAP Signavio Process Manager
  2. These models are handed off to technical teams
  3. Technical teams re-interpret and rebuild these models in execution platforms
  4. Discrepancies emerge between what was designed and what was implemented

This handoff creates friction, delays implementation, and often results in execution that doesn't fully align with the original vision.

The ability to import models from SAP Signavio Process Manager directly into SAP Build Process Automation transforms this approach. Now, the BPMN models created by business users can serve as the actual foundation for executable automation.

In addition, it is important to outline that SAP Build Process Automation will be supporting generic BPMN models import, allowing to upload BPMN diagrams from different solutions.

Please expect more details on this after the feature would be released.

Conclusion

The joint usage of SAP Signavio and SAP Build offers a powerful combination for businesses looking to optimize their processes and drive efficiency. By leveraging product recommendations, direct integration for automation, and model-to-execute capabilities, organizations can quickly identify, automate, and execute process improvements.

Please find some useful links below:

  • Official Enterprise Automation with SAP page 
  • How to get started with Enterprise Automation Guide 
  • Demo: Experience Enterprise Automation with SAP for Procure-to-Pay Processes
  • Whitepaper: Transition to SAP S/4HANA with Enterprise Automation  
  • Enterprise Automation with SAP Product Tour  
  • Enterprise Automation with SAP webinar

entry.title: Integration Insights Live: Innovate with a modern iPaaS
entry.content:
Leading businesses find the core of generating tangible outcomes from investments in enterprise AI is an integrated business landscape using integration as a service (iPaaS). Integrations make scaling AI across enterprises possible because it allows democratic information sharing across users regardless of the systems and apps used in the enterprise and no matter where the teams are located.  

Today, it is well understood that only by connecting the multitude of solutions and facilitating seamless information exchange can you truly unlock intelligence, simplify innovation, and lower your total cost of ownership. The approach of building point-to-point integrations to connect individual software systems is long gone. It lacked scale and introduced a whole new layer of complexity where every new system being added to the landscape required custom code or connectors to communicate with existing systems. Integration as a service allows you to extract maximum value from the enterprise platforms that have been invested and drive real results.

With SAP Integration Suite on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) you can easily address your integration needs using modern enterprise integration patterns such as API-based and Event-driven integrations. To learn more about its extensive functionality, join us at our upcoming virtual summit - Integration insights live: Innovate with a modern iPaaS. We’ve lined up five sessions exploring the key features, sharing real-world use cases, discussing value benefits, as well as providing an overview of the latest innovations and exciting road map.

Keynote

AI, modernization, and automation initiatives are top of mind for many IT leaders today as they grapple with increasing application landscape diversity and resource constraints. So what technologies are used to drive success of these projects? Hear Shari Lava, senior director, AI and automation  at IDC and Achim Kraiss, chief product officer for SAP Business Technology Platform discuss the strategic value of an iPaaS.

CONA Services: Accelerating innovation with SAP Integration Suite

This inspiring session features our customer CONA Services LLC, an IT services company for the 12 largest independently owned Coca-Cola bottling companies in North America. You’ll hear firsthand how the organization boosted bottlers’ business growth and competitive advantage by integrating multiple solutions and third-party applications on SAP Integration Suite.

Unleash the power of AI-driven integration for complex application landscapes

For a holistic view of integration scenarios with scalable, real-time integration of business processes, APIs, and events across SAP and third-party landscapes. I will be the speaker of this session. Do join in to learn about the comprehensive capabilities offered by SAP Integration Suite.

Connect all your applications with prebuilt integrations and adapters

If you’re struggling with a growing landscape of diverse applications and it’s making connected business processes a challenge, this session is for you! SAP Integration Suite offers hundreds of prebuilt adapters and connectors to many other applications such as those from Salesforce, ServiceNow, NetSuite, Snowflake, and more. Allowing you to connect your applications quickly, with these adapters you can avoid using multiple integration solutions for different applications.

Solving connectivity chaos to improve AI outcomes

These days, modern integration tools don’t just solve today’s complex connectivity challenges, they also enable improved AI outcomes. IDC’s Shari Lava returns to the stage to share her research findings on how modern integration platforms unlock business value from AI investments.

Don’t miss this opportunity to learn more about SAP Integration Suite and how it can support your integration needs. Whether you are looking to elevate operational efficiency or deliver digital transformation and improve AI outcomes, simplified integration is your key to success.

Register now to secure your spot. We look forward to seeing you there!

entry.title: EOIO in SAP CI Using JMS - Exclusive Mode
entry.content:
Introduction


EOIO was a long awaited functionality that every Integration consultant was waiting for in SAP CI. We had this in SAP PO where we would use it to preserve the order of messages. Messages using EOIO were stored in a FIFO queue that ensured the messages that arrive first in the middleware is also the first to leave. The order was ensured even in case of outages on the receiver system and the middleware would continue to retry the failed messages in the context of FIFO.

Solution


SAP has recently released JMS Adapter Adapter version 1.4, that has the Exclusive mode which ensures that the message order is preserved.


Both JMS Sender and Receiver adapters have a new option now - Access type - Exclusive / Non-Exclusive 

For normal scenarios where the Order in which the messages are processed doesn't matter it's highly recommended to use non-exclusive setting in Access Type. Using "Exclusive" as Access type will enforce EOIO and takes away the option of parallel processing which ultimately means that as soon as the receiver system is up messages will get processed one by one and not parallelly. 

Let's dive in to the POC now.

Here we have a integration process that has this message which is split into 5 individual messages and will be sent to a EOIO JMS Queue 

 

Content Modifier has the following message

<Root>
  <Message>
    <id>1</id>
  </Message>
  <Message>
    <id>2</id>
  </Message>
  <Message>
    <id>3</id>
  </Message>
  <Message>
    <id>4</id>
  </Message>
  <Message>
    <id>5</id>
  </Message>
</Root>

 We are splitting this and sending individual Message nodes to the JMS Queue, Parallel processing is unchecked in the splitter

  

 The Integration process runs and creates 5 messages in the JMS Queue 

Now SAP CI iflow will automatically retry these as the receiver systems URL is a dummy URL which doesn't exist

As expected only the very first message gets Retried and none of the other messages in the queue are picked up.

 

A word of caution: The way SAP PO XI adapter EOIO worked was that SAP ECC/S4 has EOIO queues as well, the EOIO Quality of service is communicated by the sender system to the middleware. In fact the backbone of EOIO on SAP PO was that the sender system itself was responsible for ensuring that messages are sent out in the exact order they are generated!

AFAIK SAP CI , XI adapter doesn't support EOIO yet! SOAP RM doesn't support it either.

If you are using a SAP CI, XI sender adapter on EO QOS in SAP S4 (as you can't use EOIO - SAP CI wont understand it) , then SAP S4/ECC in case SAP CCI is briefly unavailable will mess up the order in its internal queues! The messages will arrive to SAP CI, XI sender adapter out of order.

If you use SOAP RM Sender adapter then definitely you are not getting EOIO from the source system.

True EOIO can only be achieved if Either SAP CI is pulling the records  from SAP S4 OR the sender system ensures that messages go out in EOIO fashion even in case of an outage on the middleware or network latency.

Where can this be used

This can be used in 

1) SAP to JDBC or JDBC to SAP Scenarios
Integrations consultants use JMS queues for persistence in SAP CI via JMS Queues, If the JDBC receiver is not available then there is a risk that the messages when retried will be retried out of order. This can cause problems on the database side for some scenarios. To mitigate this using EOIO would be a great option. 


2) Integrations where data is transactional and the integration is Asynchronous and the messages are persisted in SAP CI in case of failure for automatic Retries.

These are scenarios where let's say exchange rate data is being passed from SAP to a receiver system asynchronously and the receiver system is not available and due to some reason exchange rates are fluctuating by a lot that day If out of order exchange rates are sent to the receiver system then this may cause problems. To avoid such issues in critical financial integrations EOIO in SAP CI may be an option.

That being said, this will never be true EOIO unless supported by SAP S4. 

Still This is a very welcome update from SAP. This will bridge the gap between SAP PO and SAP CI where consultants had to design workarounds to implement EOIO.

entry.title: Implementing Message Level Security: End-to-End Encryption and Decryption in SAP CPI
entry.content:
Introduction

In our previous blog, we explored the creation and configuration of PGP keys in SAP CPI. This time, we'll dive into the end-to-end implementation and working of the encryptor and decryptor, along with the sign/verify mechanism of the payload. We'll use the Postman tool to test the scenario and utilize the same keys from our previous blog on creating and managing PGP key pairs for SAP CPI integration.

Overview

This blog will cover the following steps:

  • Interface Design Implementation
  • Encrypting and Signing the Plain Text Payload
  • Decrypting and Verifying the Payload to Retrieve Plain Text

     

    Step 1: Interface Design Implementation

    First, we need to implement the interface design. This involves setting up the necessary configurations in SAP CPI to handle the encryption and decryption processes. We will use two different interfaces for encryption and decryption:

    • Encryption and Signing Interface: In the first interface, named"Z_DEMO_PGP_Encrypt_SIGN", we will encrypt and sign the data coming from Postman using the client partner's public key and our own private key.
    • Decryption and Verification Interface: In the second interface, named "ZDEMOPGPDecryptorVerifier", we will decrypt and verify the outcome of the first interface using the partner's private key and our own public key.

      Note: We don't have a configuration for the PGP decryptor pallet, so we don't need to provide the private key alias. However, the private key should be deployed in Manage PGP Keys. If the incoming payload is signed, we need to provide the public key details for verifying the signature.

      Step 2: Encrypting and Signing the Plain Text Payload

      Next, we'll use the encryptor interface to encrypt and sign the plain text payload data. This step ensures that the data is securely transmitted and can be verified for integrity. Below are the configuration details and input/output samples from Postman:

      Configuration Details:

      • Encryptor Interface: "Z_DEMO_PGP_Encrypt_SIGN"
      • Public Key Alias: PartnerDevKey  (for encryption)
      • Private Key Alias: CpiDemoKey     (for Signer)

  

  

 

 

Postman Input Sample:

Hello from Postman

Postman Output Sample:

-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----

wcBMA0cyi8mLJEMqAQf+JuSjk8UxfScdIhJt1Ginupw0pSW+9rojhYZUgFXbxrFm
MtOxGsUbGgpkIqzGp0LdVUPnv+fkjt1uvDcdK/V78CMDEpmA3TRz+Jz6c3JP7wfp
rPN7kEW8O94kPm/h34HKu+hDQMConSUnKPfnuMYLfcnYeHHPfPzwUum2531/HGou
Ap3wUyOPHcJR/IABtxlMnA0hs33xsdmRUgK9AS3kUPPt6mybLc+x7TL3JNnLNxSS
XBcBw4QYc02cnFLl984w/64U16fwiut1AuqE7FluJvlvZBNn0Xrxg3OQelg8SIFq
cZrw8pnxa3P8S7Z8VAzGTKKALcA9F0QBDjw+/N+wV9LA2AGsM8fDpInVitZRGAfk
i2kWR4eMmmxtMg7Ek8RGlZFpKWvAur3hZOp2VkSQkCaioigfkP34H6tKheWCfaIV
YaTbQvgKgN3Y15Cg8/0bVisYOjiEv9L8FMNihRmi5D7em+oOFXhtrMVRRIi33q9s
w0Nz0OYezC7jomXd01xS9GHtnk6yqhVlB+uN+2x8RzNbQbsFHG+eaP1jrnQWcVXa
3FLGsBjsXoVyPseCk1j8k6t/DMO/O/XKdUTppC3TH1W7JaTW9gozNCuogX7sHXAC
kJx/uXqlBei6cLgkUF1n53PDQLPfLwB49n12zi6eF8OVc0K9Zv6iHcKltSbFy6+R
eqQpO7Aib99Ew4unMjSMd+cJQwzSxwInkYsRECQLG19QTo+k0OamCtMWmON/mRqX
PDT/xEKdv7Cp1huEFHBoOfQmSSjN9k8fTSfIX1FihxdFbtks3yE5jHVgm4JS7Dbb
OeyWcw3n3YlTh7pBE2EOGvrMQmzUTe28V9uKVr+MJwnX1Py+yeCv44ho+z4eN0GL
3o8w5Oy1exww0Q==
=03nq
-----END PGP MESSAGE-----

 

 

 

Using Postman, we can test this scenario by sending a plain text payload to the encryptor interface and observing the encrypted and signed output.

Step 3: Decrypting and Verifying the Payload to Retrieve Plain Text

Finally, we'll send the output from the encryptor interface to the decryptor interface. This step will decrypt the payload and verify its integrity, ensuring that the original plain text data is retrieved accurately. Below are the configuration details and input/output samples from Postman:

Configuration Details:

  • Decryptor Interface: "Z_DEMO_PGP_Decryptor_Verifier"
  • Private Key Alias: No configuration step available ( it must deployed in Manage PGP Keys)
  • Public Key Alias: CpiDemoKey (for verifying the signature)

 

         In PGP decryptor's processing, we have Three options to handle Signature.

          1. None Expected.

          2. Optional.

          3. Required.

           

   Here, in content modifier add  "Below Message is decrypted and verified." with decrypted payload.

   This step is optional.

 

Postman Input Sample:

Output of previous step.

-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----

wcBMA0cyi8mLJEMqAQf+JuSjk8UxfScdIhJt1Ginupw0pSW+9rojhYZUgFXbxrFm
MtOxGsUbGgpkIqzGp0LdVUPnv+fkjt1uvDcdK/V78CMDEpmA3TRz+Jz6c3JP7wfp
rPN7kEW8O94kPm/h34HKu+hDQMConSUnKPfnuMYLfcnYeHHPfPzwUum2531/HGou
Ap3wUyOPHcJR/IABtxlMnA0hs33xsdmRUgK9AS3kUPPt6mybLc+x7TL3JNnLNxSS
XBcBw4QYc02cnFLl984w/64U16fwiut1AuqE7FluJvlvZBNn0Xrxg3OQelg8SIFq
cZrw8pnxa3P8S7Z8VAzGTKKALcA9F0QBDjw+/N+wV9LA2AGsM8fDpInVitZRGAfk
i2kWR4eMmmxtMg7Ek8RGlZFpKWvAur3hZOp2VkSQkCaioigfkP34H6tKheWCfaIV
YaTbQvgKgN3Y15Cg8/0bVisYOjiEv9L8FMNihRmi5D7em+oOFXhtrMVRRIi33q9s
w0Nz0OYezC7jomXd01xS9GHtnk6yqhVlB+uN+2x8RzNbQbsFHG+eaP1jrnQWcVXa
3FLGsBjsXoVyPseCk1j8k6t/DMO/O/XKdUTppC3TH1W7JaTW9gozNCuogX7sHXAC
kJx/uXqlBei6cLgkUF1n53PDQLPfLwB49n12zi6eF8OVc0K9Zv6iHcKltSbFy6+R
eqQpO7Aib99Ew4unMjSMd+cJQwzSxwInkYsRECQLG19QTo+k0OamCtMWmON/mRqX
PDT/xEKdv7Cp1huEFHBoOfQmSSjN9k8fTSfIX1FihxdFbtks3yE5jHVgm4JS7Dbb
OeyWcw3n3YlTh7pBE2EOGvrMQmzUTe28V9uKVr+MJwnX1Py+yeCv44ho+z4eN0GL
3o8w5Oy1exww0Q==
=03nq
-----END PGP MESSAGE-----

Postman Output Sample:

Below Message is decrypted and verified.
Hello from Postman

Using Postman, we can verify that the decrypted data matches the original payload, confirming the process works correctly.

Conclusion

By following these steps, you can successfully implement and test the encryptor and decryptor mechanisms in SAP CPI. This ensures secure and verified data transmission, leveraging the PGP keys created earlier. Stay tuned for more insights and detailed implementations in our upcoming blogs!

 

Feel free to leave any questions, suggestions, or feedback in the comments section below. We'd love to hear from you!

entry.title: SAP Cloud Connector 2.18 is out!
entry.content:
We’re excited to announce the availability of SAP Cloud Connector version 2.18. Building on previous iterations, this latest version offers several technical enhancements and solutions aimed at streamlining operations. Here are the key updates:

This release introduces support for Windows Server 2025. If you're preparing to upgrade your infrastructure, now’s the time to explore this compatibility. Note that some older OS versions will no longer be supported, so check the Product Availability Matrix for precise details.

Certificates are crucial for maintaining secure connections, but they come with expiration dates. As frequently requested by our users, the new version supports automatic renewal for subaccounts, minimizing the risk of downtime due to forgotten refreshes.

Don’t forget to enable Certificate refresh in the BTP Cockpit. This capability isn't available in all cloud regions immediately; it will be rolled out in phases across different regions. Once it becomes available in your region, the toggle button in the BTP Cockpit will be visible.

The administration UI defaults to Morning Horizon theme. However, you can also choose from other Horizon options, including Evening Horizon and Black/White High Contrast variants.

Navigate to Configuration > User Interface to set your preferred theme.

Version 2.18 introduces centralized management of E-mail and LDAP server trust stores directly within the Cloud Connector, bypassing the traditional JDK trust store. This setup allows finer control over securing backend communications with trusted certificates.

A new Troubleshooting section proactively diagnoses problems within the Cloud Connector.

 You’ll find detailed reports on issues, especially those affecting data tunnels, along with root cause analyses and potential fixes. The framework categorizes and tracks recurring issues, providing insights into their recurrence patterns over time.

The cross-subaccount performance monitor aggregates metrics from all subaccounts related to cloud-to-on-premises traffic. 

This consolidation aids in a more comprehensive analysis, helping you optimize resource allocation and network configurations efficiently.

Configure a fadeout time for service channels in HA setups to ensure smoother transitions when roles change intentionally between master and shadow instances. Known as the Service Channel Termination Grace Period, this new feature minimizes service disruptions by maintaining active channels during switches.

Certificate-based authentication now has a fallback mechanism to revert to user/password authentication if necessary. This added flexibility in logon processes ensures continual access, even when certificate-related issues arise.

Overall, SAP Cloud Connector 2.18 delivers enhancements focused on facilitating better security management, improving performance insights, and increasing reliability in high-stakes environments. Upgrade today to leverage these new features. For all technical details, see our comprehensive release notes.

Happy connecting!

entry.title: Secure Kafka Integration on SAP Integration Suite: Advantco Kafka Private Link Proxy with Confluent
entry.content:
Introduction

As enterprises adopt real-time, event-driven architectures, Apache Kafka plays a central role in managing high-throughput data streams. Integrating Kafka with SAP Integration Suite opens new possibilities for event-driven business processes—but doing this securely in the cloud can be a challenge.

By combining the Advantco Kafka Adapter for SAP CPI with the Private Link Service on SAP BTP, organizations can securely integrate SAP Integration Suite with Confluent Cloud Kafka clusters, leveraging private networking on AWS and Azure. This approach avoids exposing services over the public internet, meeting strict security and compliance requirements.

 

The Integration Challenge

In traditional setups, Kafka clients—like SAP Integration Suite —must connect directly to Kafka brokers over public endpoints. This connection typically relies on public DNS resolution, meaning the broker’s IP or hostname is exposed on the internet.

For on-premise Kafka installations, customers often configure SAP Cloud Connector to allow SAP CPI tenants to reach Kafka brokers securely. However, this requires additional setup and complexity, especially in hybrid environments.

Confluent Cloud, a fully managed Kafka service, simplifies event streaming. When deployed with Private Networking, it allows private, secure communication between your infrastructure and Confluent Cloud clusters—without public exposure.

 

Enter SAP Private Link Service + Advantco Kafka Adapter

When running Confluent Cloud Kafka clusters with Private Link Service enabled, direct communication between SAP CPI and the Kafka brokers isn’t possible—since CPI tenants can’t reach private endpoints directly.

To overcome this, Advantco provides a Kafka BTP Application, deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry, which acts as an intermediary (proxy). It securely forwards requests from the Advantco Kafka Adapter (on SAP Integration Suite) to the Kafka broker via SAP Private Link Service.

Why Private Link Service Matters

Without Private Link:

  • SAP Integration Suite would need to connect over the public internet
  • Potential compliance and security issues arise
  • More complicated networking (VPNs, NATs, etc.)

With Private Link + Advantco BTP App:

  • Private, direct, secure communication
  • No traffic ever leaves the cloud provider’s internal network
  • Simpler, cleaner network architecture

Key Benefits of This Approach

  • Secure Connectivity: No public internet exposure, reducing risk.
  •  Low Latency & High Performance: Direct, private links reduce network hops.
  • Compliance-Friendly: Meets SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR standards.
  • Multi-Cloud & Hybrid Ready: Supports AWS, Azure, and GCP with SAP BTP.
  • Simplified Network Management: No need for VPNs or NAT gateways.

 

Solution Architecture Overview

  1. Advantco Kafka Adapter (SAP Integration Suite)
    • Sends produce requests to Advantco Kafka BTP App
    • Cannot directly access PrivateLink-enabled brokers
  2. Advantco Kafka BTP Application (SAP BTP Cloud Foundry)
    • Acts as the bridge between SAP Integration Suite and Kafka
    • Deployed in a BTP space with SAP Private Link enabled
    • Forwards requests to Kafka brokers securely
  3. SAP Private Link Service
    • Provides private connectivity from SAP BTP to cloud providers
    • Ensures communication with Kafka brokers over private endpoints only
  4. Confluent Cloud Kafka (Private Networking Enabled)
    • Kafka cluster runs in AWS or Azure, accessible via PrivateLink endpoints only
    • No public IP addresses or DNS records are used

Picture 1: Architecture Diagram of the Kafka Private Link Proxy Service on SAP BTP

Flow description:

- Advantco Kafka adapter: an SAP Integration Suite adapter with Private Link Service enabled.

Picture 2: Configure of the Advantco Kafka channel to use Private Link.

- Advantco Kafka Private Link Proxy is an application deployed on BTP Cloud Foundry Space used to receive requests from Advantco Kafka adapter on SAP Integration Suite.

Picture 3: The Advantco Kafka Private Link Proxy application in SAP BTP

- SAP Private Link Service: enables secure, private communication between SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) without exposing traffic to the public internet.

- Confluent Cloud with Private Networking: a private cluster bound with Cloud Provider’s Private Link Service. The cluster can be accessed through Private Endpoint only.

 

AWS and Azure Deployment Scenarios

AWS Deployment

  • Confluent Cloud on AWS provides PrivateLink endpoints in the customer’s AWS VPC
  • SAP BTP on AWS connects via SAP Private Link Service to those PrivateLink endpoints
  • Kafka clients (Advantco Kafka BTP App) connect securely over AWS’s internal network
  • PrivateLink setup requested and managed through Confluent Cloud Console
  • Requires SAP BTP and Kafka broker to be in the same or peered AWS region

Azure Deployment

  • Confluent Cloud on Azure provides Private Endpoints in a VNet
  • SAP BTP on Azure uses SAP Private Link Service to connect to those Private Endpoints
  • Kafka clients (Advantco Kafka BTP App) connect securely via Microsoft’s private network
  • Private Networking enabled and configured via Confluent Cloud Console
  • Requires SAP BTP and Kafka broker to be in the same Azure region

 

Conclusion

By leveraging Advantco Kafka Adapter, SAP Private Link Service, and Confluent Cloud Private Networking, businesses can securely integrate Kafka into their SAP Integration Suite based processes without compromising performance or compliance.

entry.title: Introduction to Group Management in SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh
entry.content:
With the latest enhancement of SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh, the Group Management feature is now available. This feature simplifies the user access management to AEM by providing a access grouping mechanism for external identify services (e.g. SAP IAS) . In this blog, I will explain step by step how to set up and use Cloud Group Management.

Why Group Management?

Previously, administrators had to manually create user & user roles within SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh, which quickly became consuming and not always aligned with customer user access guidelines . With Cloud Group Management, you can logically group access permissions, optimizing management and security settings.

 

Step-by-Step Guide to Setup

1. Create a Cloud Group

  1. Log in to the to your Advanced Event Mesh service.

  2. Navigate to Account Details, User Management to User Groups section.

  3. Click on Create New Cloud Group and define the groups you would like to use

In my case I have created a "Viewer" role only, which will allow users to access the Event Portal, the Broker settings and the Datadog, but not to perform any changes.

2. Manage Claim Mapping

After you create user groups, you can map claims from your IdP to the groups. Align claims from your identity provider to your available user groups to automatically assign roles to group members. 

  1. Navigate to Group Management 

  2. Add Claim_Value and the defined role from section 1

(Optional) Select Enable Just-In-Time provisioning to allow users who are able to authenticate with the identity provider to log in to Advanced Event Mesh and be added to a group without being manually invited first.(Optional) Select Customize Default User Group and select a user group from the Default User Group drop-down list. When selected, if no defined claim mappings match the user claim value, the user is added to the specified group. When not selected, if no claim value for the user is mapped to a user group, the user is denied access to advanced event mesh.

3. Manage User & Permissions in IAS

  1. In SAP IAS, ensure that you create a group with exactly the same group name defined in AEM.

  2. Assign group to users, which should get "Viewer" access. In my case I take a personal mail address.

4. Access and Check the role

Time to test, access your AEM application. 

And double check your account and the assigned roles

 

With Cloud Group Management in SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh, managing user access becomes significantly more efficient. Groups simplify organization, enhance security, and reduce administrative effort. Try it out and integrate it into your event-driven architecture!

Do you have any questions or comments? Feel free to share them in the comments!

entry.title: Increase JMS queue size in Integration Suite
entry.content:
This blog describes how to increase JMS queue size in Integration suite. A JMS queue size refers to the capacity of the queue to hold messages. This size can determine how many messages can be stored in the queue before any additional incoming messages are either rejected or cause alternative handling strategies like scaling or persistence to come into play.

Increasing the size of a JMS queue may be necessary for several reasons, primarily related to performance, reliability, and scalability.

1. Access integration suite tenant, click on "Integration suite" tab displayed on the webui as shown in the below screenshot:

2. You will be navigated to capabilities page:

3. Scroll up & click on manage capabilities:

4. In Manage capabilities, you will see "Message queue details", click on edit:

5. Increase queue size up to maximum 100 and save the changes:

 

6. To verify the same, navigate to Monitoring > Manage message queues

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - Message Status Overview by Sender & Receiver
entry.content:
When I first started using CPI, I missed a lot the monitoring capabilities of SAP PO… A few months ago, SAP released the Message Status Overview functionality for SAP Integration Suite and it  made me really happy, but I still miss the ability to filter by Business Component by standard. I usually like to filter in SAP PO by SAP systems to know if all messages from/to SAP have been processed successfully:

To achieve this in SAP Integration Suite, it is necessary to fill in SAP_Sender and SAP_Receiver message headers in all integration flows. You only need to create the message headers in a Content Modifier step:

Now, you will see data in the Message Status Overview by selecting the “Overview By Senders” or “Overview By Receivers” option:

It is easy, but it is necessary to define a clear procedure for developers: Maintain a system catalog and make sure to add the headers in every iflow. Also, if there is more than one receiver, you have to decide what to do because the SAP_Receiver header is unique for each message.

By default, the different receivers are concatenated and stored together in the SAP_Receiver header. This is not very useful because in the overview each combination behaves as an separete Receiver:

An alternative is to store only the main receiver system of the process, but in some cases the main receiver may not be clear and this overview may not give you the real situation of your messages.

The other option is to use the property (not message header!) SAP_ReceiverOverwrite. By setting this property to true at the beginning of the process, the SAP_Receiver header is overwritten during the process execution. With this option you could also lose information, but for error messages you will have a more realistic situation.

In conclusion, there is a way in SAP Integration Suite to filter by Sender and Receiver systems in the style of SAP PO, but it requires effort. I hope SAP will improve how to manage and select systems in integration flows.
